QF.id: Income, Residential Status, Five Heads of Income_10

question.text: Which of the following conditions makes a Resident individual an RNOR (Resident but Not Ordinarily Resident)?

answer.text: The individual has been resident in India for 9 out of 10 preceding previous years
The individual has been a non-resident in India in 9 out of the 10 preceding previous years
The individual has stayed 730 or more days in India during the 7 preceding previous years
The individual's Indian income exceeds Rs.15 lakh and he has stayed 182 days in India

explanation.text: **The Logic:** A Resident qualifies as RNOR if ANY ONE of the following is satisfied: (a) NR in 9 out of 10 preceding previous years, OR (b) total stay in India ≤729 days in 7 preceding previous years, OR (c) deemed resident under s.6(1A). Option B correctly states the NR-in-9/10-years condition. Option A reverses it (resident in 9/10 — that would make them ROR, not RNOR). Option C states 730+ days — the threshold is ≤729 days (i.e., less than 730). Option D describes a residency condition, not an RNOR condition. **The Trap:** The 9-out-of-10 test is frequently reversed by examiners — "resident in 9/10" vs "non-resident in 9/10" are opposites. Similarly, candidates confuse ≤729 with ≥730. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM X-B frequently tests the precise RNOR conditions. The distinction between ROR and RNOR matters greatly for scope of taxable income. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** RNOR status is a valuable tax planning window for returning NRIs — their foreign income remains untaxed in India during RNOR years. Help clients track their year-count carefully.
